editorial.title: The nocturnal terror: an open letter to the eastern koel
editorial.teaser: While the northern beaches council argues over housing and developers, a far more pressing crisis is unfolding in our backyards: the relentless, escalating scream of a bird looking for love in all the wrong time zones.
editorial.body:
It starts low. A tentative, almost polite *coo-ee*.

you check the digital clock glowing in the dark: 2:14 am. You turn over, adjusting the pillow to the cool side.

then, it happens again. *coo-ee*.

but this time, with a rising inflection. It is the eastern koel, the feathered sociopath of the australian suburbs, and he has decided that the gum tree directly outside your bedroom window is the main stage at the sydney opera house.

science tells us this is a mating call. Biology suggests this male bird is attempting to woo a female by proving his lung capacity and persistence.

however, as a man of logic, i posit a different theory: this is psychological warfare. The koel does not want a mate; it wants to drive the local real estate prices down by inducing mass sleep deprivation.

the cadence is what breaks the human spirit. It is not a rhythmic lullaby; it is a panic attack set to music.

it starts slow, then accelerates—*ko-el, ko-el, ko-el, ko-el!*—until it reaches a fever pitch of maniacal desperation, only to stop abruptly, leaving you in a silence so heavy you can hear your own blood pressure rising. Just as you drift off?

he begins the second movement. We worry about the cost of living, or the latest political scandals in canberra, but those are daylight problems.

the koel is the darkness made audible. He is a migratory parasite (literally, they take over other birds' nests) who winters in the north and returns solely to ruin our spring.

there is no solution. You cannot find him; he is a ventriloquist in camouflage, a red-eyed ghost in the foliage.

you can only lie there, staring at the ceiling, praying for a thunderstorm, realizing that for all our technological advancements, we are entirely at the mercy of a bird with the libido of a rock star and the vocal projection of a foghorn.
editorial.author: Professor Remy
